## Walkthrough

This pull request replaces the Prisma ORM with Drizzle ORM across the application. The migration includes: removing the Prisma schema and seed infrastructure; adding Drizzle configuration and a new PostgreSQL schema definition; rewriting seed functions to use Drizzle APIs; updating database initialization to instantiate a Drizzle client with a `pg` connection pool; migrating all data-access routers to use Drizzle query builders and insert/update/delete operations; switching the Better Auth adapter from Prisma to Drizzle; replacing error handling to recognize PostgreSQL-specific error codes; updating all unit tests to mock Drizzle's query/mutation API shape; updating package.json scripts from `prisma` commands to `drizzle-kit` equivalents; removing Prisma dependencies and adding Drizzle packages; and improving TypeScript type annotations in form components and UI wrappers.

## Possibly related PRs

- **hbmartin/start-ui-web#1** — Performs the identical Prisma-to-Drizzle ORM migration across the same codebase layers: schema, database client, routers, tests, seed functions, and build configuration.

## Code Review

This pull request migrates the project from Prisma to Drizzle ORM, updating the database schema, seeding scripts, authentication, and routers, alongside a major dependency overhaul. A bug was identified in the database initialization where a missing $client property causes the seed script to crash during cleanup. Additionally, the cursor pagination in the book and user routers is susceptible to failure if records are deleted, suggesting that sort values should be encoded into the cursor for improved robustness.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

This manual cursor pagination implementation is not robust to record deletions. If the item corresponding to `input.cursor` is deleted between requests, `cursorRow` will be `undefined`, causing the `cursorFilter` to be bypassed and the pagination to restart from the first page. Consider encoding the sort values (e.g., `title` and `id`) into the cursor string itself (e.g., as a base64 encoded JSON) to avoid the need to fetch the cursor row from the database.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

Similar to the book router, this cursor pagination implementation will restart from the first page if the cursor record is deleted. Encoding the sort values into the cursor string would make this more robust.
